title: Terminal AI — Copilot CLI: The Who, the How, and the Why

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you will install, configure, and master the new GitHub Copilot CLI, a fully agentic AI assistant that runs directly in your terminal.

Your challenge: complete a series of real-world terminal tasks using Copilot CLI. By the end, you will have launched interactive sessions, used plan mode, managed tool approvals, connected to GitHub, and built productivity aliases.

Follow the steps in this panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Verify Node.js Version
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and check that Node.js 22 or later is installed:

node --version

If the version is below 22, update Node.js before continuing.

steps.title: Verify PowerShell Version (Windows)
steps.instruction:
If you are on Windows, confirm PowerShell v6 or higher:

$PSVersionTable.PSVersion

If below v6, install PowerShell 7 from winget install Microsoft.PowerShell.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Install Copilot CLI
steps.instruction:
Install Copilot CLI globally with npm:

npm install -g @github/copilot

This makes the copilot command available in any terminal session.

steps.title: Verify Installation
steps.instruction:
Confirm the installation succeeded:

copilot --version

You should see a version number. If the command is not found, close and reopen your terminal, then try again.
steps.tip: To update Copilot CLI later, run the same install command: <kbd>npm install -g @github/copilot</kbd>.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Launch Copilot CLI
steps.instruction:
Navigate to a project directory you trust, then launch Copilot CLI:

copilot

Copilot will ask you to confirm that you trust the current directory. Choose Yes, proceed to trust it for this session only.

steps.title: Authenticate
steps.instruction:
If prompted to authenticate, use the /login slash command and follow the on-screen instructions:

/login

Once authenticated, you should see Copilot's welcome message and prompt.
steps.tip: If you are already authenticated via the GitHub CLI (gh), Copilot CLI may pick up your existing session.

steps.title: Exit the Session
steps.instruction:
For now, exit the session so we can explore features one at a time:

Press Ctrl+C or type /exit

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Start an Interactive Session
steps.instruction:
Launch a new Copilot CLI session:

copilot

Trust the directory when prompted, then try a simple request:

What files are in this directory?

Copilot will read your directory and respond. Notice how it requests approval before using any tools.

steps.title: Ask a Follow-Up
steps.instruction:
The session is conversational. Ask a follow-up question based on the previous response:

For example: Which of those files was modified most recently?

You can also include a specific file with the @ prefix: Explain @package.json (Tab completion supported).

steps.title: Steer While Thinking
steps.instruction:
Try sending a message while Copilot is still processing a response. You can queue additional instructions or redirect the conversation without waiting for it to finish.

This is a powerful feature for staying in control of complex interactions.
steps.tip: When Copilot is thinking, you can type additional messages that it will process after completing its current response.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Switch to Plan Mode
steps.instruction:
Start a Copilot CLI session (or continue your existing one). Press Shift+Tab to switch to plan mode (you will see the mode indicator change).

Now submit a multi-step request:

Create a simple Node.js script that reads a JSON file and prints a formatted summary

Instead of immediately writing code, Copilot will analyze the request and build a plan for your review.

steps.title: Review the Plan
steps.instruction:
Examine the plan Copilot presents. Check whether it matches your intent:

• Does it create the right files?
• Does it use the approach you expected?
• Are there any steps you would change?

Approve the plan to let Copilot proceed, or provide feedback to adjust it.
steps.tip: Press <kbd>Shift+Tab</kbd> again to cycle to autopilot mode, then again to return to standard mode. Each mode is suited to different levels of trust and task complexity.

steps.title: Explore Autopilot Mode
steps.instruction:
Press Shift+Tab until you reach autopilot mode. In this mode, Copilot works through multi-step tasks with greater autonomy.

For a quick test, try: List all files in this directory and tell me what each one does

Notice that Copilot proceeds through tool calls without asking for individual approval.
steps.tip: On the command line, use --autopilot and --max-autopilot-continues=N to control autonomous runs.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Trigger a Tool Approval
steps.instruction:
In your Copilot CLI session, ask it to do something that requires file access:

Create a file called hello.txt with the text "Hello from Copilot CLI"

When Copilot asks for tool approval, choose Yes (option 1) to approve this single action.

steps.title: Try Rejecting an Action
steps.instruction:
Now ask Copilot to do something, and when the tool approval appears, choose No, and tell Copilot what to do differently (Esc).

Tell Copilot to use a different approach. For example: Don't create a new file. Instead, print the message to the terminal.

This demonstrates how you can redirect Copilot without stopping the conversation.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Try Slash Commands
steps.instruction:
In your Copilot CLI session, try these slash commands:

• /context to see your token usage breakdown
• /model to view available models (you can select a different one)
• /mcp to see connected MCP servers

These commands give you visibility into your session's state.

steps.title: Compact the Context
steps.instruction:
If you have been chatting for a while, try:

/compact

This manually compresses your conversation history to free up context space. Copilot also does this automatically at 95% capacity, enabling virtually unlimited sessions.
steps.tip: Press <kbd>Esc</kbd> to cancel a compaction if you change your mind.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Programmatic Mode
steps.instruction:
Exit your interactive session if one is running (Ctrl+C or /exit). Then try programmatic mode:

copilot -p "What is the current date and time?"

Copilot processes the request and exits. No ongoing session.

steps.title: Use Approval Flags
steps.instruction:
Try programmatic mode with a tool approval flag:

copilot -p "Show me this week's git commits and summarize them" --allow-tool='shell(git)'

The --allow-tool flag lets Copilot run git commands without prompting. Try modifying the flag to allow other tools.
steps.tip: Use --deny-tool='shell(rm)' alongside --allow-all-tools to allow everything except destructive delete commands.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Local Task: Code Modification
steps.instruction:
Start a Copilot CLI session and ask it to make a change to your project:

copilot

Then try something like:

Suggest improvements to the README in this project

Watch how Copilot reads the file, analyzes it, and proposes specific changes. Approve or reject each tool action as you see fit.

steps.title: Local Task: Git Operations
steps.instruction:
Try a Git operation through Copilot:

Show me the last 5 commits with a brief summary of each

Then try: What files have changed since the last commit?

Copilot executes Git commands on your behalf and presents the results in a readable format.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: GitHub.com Task
steps.instruction:
In your Copilot CLI session, try interacting with GitHub.com via the pre-configured MCP server:

List my open pull requests

Or try: List all open issues in this repository

Copilot uses the GitHub MCP server to fetch data directly from GitHub.com.

steps.title: Challenge: Create an Issue
steps.instruction:
If you have a repository where you are comfortable creating a test issue, try:

Create an issue titled "Test: Copilot CLI lab exercise" with a description explaining this is a test

If you prefer not to create a real issue, try listing existing issues or pull requests instead.
steps.tip: If you know that a specific MCP server can handle a task, mention it in your prompt for better results.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: CLI for Terminal Tasks
steps.instruction:
With the Terminal open, use Copilot CLI for a terminal-centric task:

copilot -p "What is the total size of this project directory?" --allow-tool='shell'

Now open Copilot Chat in VS Code and ask it a code-centric question about the same project. Notice how each tool brings different strengths.

steps.title: Compare the Tools
steps.instruction:
Try asking the same question to both tools and compare the results:

• In the Terminal, ask Copilot CLI about the project structure
• In Copilot Chat, ask about the same project using @workspace

Each tool provides a different perspective based on its capabilities.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create Aliases
steps.instruction:
Set up shortcut aliases for this session:

PowerShell:
function cpcli { copilot -p @args }
function cpall { copilot --allow-all-tools -p @args }

Bash/Zsh:
alias cpcli='copilot -p'
alias cpall='copilot --allow-all-tools -p'

Test them: cpcli "What is the current date?"

steps.title: Test Your Aliases
steps.instruction:
Verify your aliases work with a few quick commands:

• cpcli "List all running processes"
• cpall "Show me the git log for the last 3 commits"

If the aliases work, you have reduced the friction to near zero.
steps.tip: To make aliases permanent, add them to your shell profile: $PROFILE for PowerShell, ~/.bashrc for bash, or ~/.zshrc for zsh.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Prompt Specificity Challenge
steps.instruction:
Test the impact of prompt specificity. Start an interactive session with copilot, then compare two requests:

• Vague: Tell me about this project
• Specific: Read the package.json and tell me what dependencies this project uses, what the main entry point is, and what scripts are available

Compare the depth and usefulness of each response.

steps.title: Plan Mode for Complex Tasks
steps.instruction:
Switch to plan mode (Shift+Tab) and try a complex, multi-step request:

Analyze this project's test coverage, identify areas with no tests, and create a plan to add basic test coverage for the most critical files

Review the plan Copilot builds. This is the workflow to use for any task where you want to verify the approach before execution.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! You have completed Copilot CLI: The Who, the How, and the Why.

You now know how to:
• Install Copilot CLI with npm install -g @github/copilot
• Launch interactive sessions and navigate trusted directory prompts
• Use plan mode for complex, multi-step tasks
• Manage tool approval to stay in control
• Use slash commands to manage sessions, models, and context
• Run programmatic mode with approval flags for automation
• Interact with GitHub.com via the built-in MCP server
• Combine CLI and VS Code Chat for full coverage
• Build aliases for near-zero friction

Next courses: Enterprise-Grade Marmalade for governance, MCP Maximizer for advanced MCP, or Agent Potato: License to Chat for Copilot agent mode.
